---
title: "03 · Policies as Conditional Distributions"
subtitle: "A policy is the agent's strategy: a conditional distribution over actions given the current state."
lesson-id: "mfrl.policy"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.state-transitions"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "game-dev"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "drafted"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest. Are you ready for this lesson?

1. From [Lesson 2](../02-state-transitions/index.qmd), can you write
   $P(s' \mid s, a)$ as a row of a probability table?
2. Are you comfortable with **summing a probability distribution over its
   outcomes** to get $1$?
3. Could you draw an arrow from each cell of a 3×3 grid telling the agent
   which way to go?

If a "no" appears, glance back at lesson 2 before continuing.
:::

## Touch

You have a 3×3 grid in front of you. The world is fixed (you have a
transition kernel $P$). Now imagine writing instructions for a robot that
will live in this world.

**Strategy A — straight arrows.** On each cell, draw a single arrow. "If
you find yourself in $(0, 0)$, go *right*. If $(0, 1)$, go *right*. If
$(0, 2)$, go *down* …". One arrow per cell. Clean and predictable.

**Strategy B — pie charts.** On each cell, draw a small pie chart showing
the probability of picking each direction. "$(1, 1)$: 60% right, 20% down,
10% up, 10% left." On any visit to $(1, 1)$ the robot rolls a 4-sided
weighted die and acts accordingly.

```{ojs}
viewof piePolicyCell = Inputs.radio(["A — arrows", "B — pies"], {value: "A — arrows", label: "Policy style"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const isPie = piePolicyCell.startsWith("B");
  return html`<div style="font-family: sans-serif; padding: 0.5rem 0;">
    <strong>${isPie ? "Stochastic policy" : "Deterministic policy"}</strong>:
    ${isPie
      ? "each cell carries a probability over actions; the agent rolls weighted dice."
      : "each cell carries one chosen action; the agent has no choice to make at runtime."}
  </div>`;
}
```

Caption: switch between deterministic (Strategy A) and stochastic (Strategy
B) policy styles.

Both A and B are *policies*. The first is a special case of the second.

## Wonder

1. Is "always go right" a valid policy on a 3×3 grid? What if "right" is
   sometimes blocked by a wall — does the policy break?
2. Why might a *random* policy ever be better than a *fixed* one?
3. Once you fix a policy, the world has no more "decisions" left in it.
   What does each turn become?

## Discover

### Problem 1 — A function from states to actions

Write down a deterministic policy on the corridor $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$ with
$A = \{L, R\}$ that pushes the agent toward cell 3. Write it as a
*function* $\pi : S \to A$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A function gives one output per input. List the three outputs.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi(1) = R, \pi(2) = R, \pi(3) = R$ (or $\pi(3) = L$ — either works since
3 is the goal). The whole policy is three arrows.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Allow randomness

Now write a stochastic policy: $\pi(a \mid s)$, the probability of picking
action $a$ in state $s$. Pick a non-trivial example for the corridor and
verify $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) = 1$ for each $s$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each state needs a small probability distribution over actions.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Example: $\pi(R \mid 1) = 0.9$, $\pi(L \mid 1) = 0.1$;
$\pi(R \mid 2) = 0.7$, $\pi(L \mid 2) = 0.3$;
$\pi(R \mid 3) = 0.5$, $\pi(L \mid 3) = 0.5$.
Each row sums to $1$.

A *deterministic* policy is the special case where each row puts mass $1$
on a single action.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Plug a policy into the world

The world has $P(s' \mid s, a)$. The agent has $\pi(a \mid s)$. What is the
probability that, starting at $s$, you end at $s'$ in *one step*?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You don't know which $a$ will be chosen — sum over actions.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$P^\pi(s' \mid s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \cdot P(s' \mid s, a)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$P^\pi(s' \mid s) = \sum_{a \in A} \pi(a \mid s) \cdot P(s' \mid s, a).$$
The action drops out by averaging — only the *(state, next-state)* pair
remains. We have collapsed the MDP into a Markov chain.
</details>

### Problem 4 — A policy plus an MDP equals a Markov chain

Notice the formula in Problem 3 has no $a$ on the left and no $a$ inside
$P^\pi$. What kind of object is $P^\pi$? Why does that make every
question about long-run behaviour easier?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$P^\pi(s' \mid s)$ depends only on $(s, s')$. That is the shape of...?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P^\pi(s' \mid s)$ is the transition kernel of a *plain Markov chain* on
$S$. The decision-making has been integrated out. From now on, "what
happens under $\pi$" is a question about a Markov chain — for which we have
a century of theorems (stationary distributions, mixing times, hitting
probabilities, Perron–Frobenius). The collapse is what makes value
functions tractable.
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
**Policy + MDP = Markov chain.** A policy is the missing ingredient that
turns "an environment with a decision at every step" into "a stochastic
process that just runs." Everything else in this track — value functions,
Bellman equations, convergence theorems — operates on this collapsed
chain. Pick a policy, and the world flows on its own.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **policy** is a conditional distribution
$$\pi(a \mid s) \ge 0, \quad \sum_{a \in A} \pi(a \mid s) = 1, \quad \forall s \in S.$$

A *deterministic* policy puts probability $1$ on a single action at each
state. The **policy-induced transition kernel** is
$$P^\pi(s' \mid s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s)\,P(s' \mid s, a),$$
turning the MDP into a Markov chain on $S$ alone.

:::

A verse to remember it by:

> *State to action, with weights that sum to one;*
> *roll the die, the world flows on.*
> *Policy in the kernel, kernel in the chain —*
> *what was a choice becomes a lane.*

## Apply

### Worked example — induced chain on a corridor

Take the corridor $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$ with actions $A = \{L, R\}$ and slip:

| Action | Effect (deterministic on the corridor's edges) |
|--------|----------|
| $R$ at $s$ | $P(\min(s+1, 3) \mid s, R) = 0.8$, $P(s \mid s, R) = 0.2$ |
| $L$ at $s$ | $P(\max(s-1, 1) \mid s, L) = 0.8$, $P(s \mid s, L) = 0.2$ |

Take the stochastic policy $\pi(R \mid s) = 0.9$, $\pi(L \mid s) = 0.1$ for
every $s$.

**Step 1 — compute $P^\pi(s' \mid s = 2)$.** From state 2:

- Under $a = R$: $P(3 \mid 2, R) = 0.8$, $P(2 \mid 2, R) = 0.2$.
- Under $a = L$: $P(1 \mid 2, L) = 0.8$, $P(2 \mid 2, L) = 0.2$.

Average over $\pi$:

$$P^\pi(3 \mid 2) = 0.9 \cdot 0.8 + 0.1 \cdot 0 = 0.72,$$
$$P^\pi(1 \mid 2) = 0.9 \cdot 0 + 0.1 \cdot 0.8 = 0.08,$$
$$P^\pi(2 \mid 2) = 0.9 \cdot 0.2 + 0.1 \cdot 0.2 = 0.20.$$

**Step 2 — verify the row sums to 1.** $0.72 + 0.08 + 0.20 = 1.00$. Good.

**Step 3 — read the result.** Under this policy, from cell 2 you head
right with probability $0.72$, sit still with $0.20$, and drift left with
$0.08$. The MDP has been replaced by a 3-state Markov chain.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** On the corridor, write the *uniformly random* policy
$\pi(a \mid s)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi(R \mid s) = \pi(L \mid s) = 0.5$ for every $s$. Each row sums to 1.
</details>

**W2.** Write the deterministic "go right always" policy as a stochastic
$\pi(a \mid s)$ table. Confirm rows sum to 1.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi(R \mid s) = 1, \pi(L \mid s) = 0$ for every $s$. Each row $1 + 0 = 1$.
</details>

**W3.** From the worked example, what is $P^\pi(2 \mid 1)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Apply the same averaging formula at $s = 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
At $s = 1$: under $R$, $P(2 \mid 1, R) = 0.8$, $P(1 \mid 1, R) = 0.2$.
Under $L$, $P(1 \mid 1, L) = 1$ (you can't go below 1).
$P^\pi(2 \mid 1) = 0.9 \cdot 0.8 + 0.1 \cdot 0 = 0.72$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A 2-state MDP $\{X, Y\}$ with two actions $\{a, b\}$. Kernel:
$P(X \mid X, a) = 1$, $P(Y \mid X, b) = 1$, $P(Y \mid Y, a) = 1$,
$P(X \mid Y, b) = 1$. Take the policy $\pi(a \mid s) = 0.5$, $\pi(b \mid s) = 0.5$
for every $s$. Compute the full $P^\pi$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two rows, two columns. Average each (state, next-state) entry over $\pi$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P^\pi(X \mid X) = 0.5 \cdot 1 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 0.5$,
$P^\pi(Y \mid X) = 0.5 \cdot 0 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5$.
$P^\pi(Y \mid Y) = 0.5 \cdot 1 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 0.5$,
$P^\pi(X \mid Y) = 0.5 \cdot 0 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5$.
The induced chain is the symmetric 2-state coin-flip chain.
</details>

**P2.** Same MDP, but a *deterministic* policy $\pi(X) = a, \pi(Y) = b$.
What does $P^\pi$ look like? Describe the induced dynamics in words.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P^\pi(X \mid X) = 1$, $P^\pi(X \mid Y) = 1$, others 0.

In words: from $X$ you stay at $X$ forever; from $Y$ you jump to $X$ on
the next step and stay. The chain absorbs at $X$. A choice of policy can
turn a chain that "bounces around" (P1) into one that "settles down"
(this).
</details>

**P3.** *Why randomness sometimes helps.* Consider the MDP from P1 with a
reward $R(X, b, Y) = 1$ and zero otherwise. Compare the long-run reward
rate under (i) the always-$a$ policy and (ii) the always-$b$ policy.
Which is better, and why might a *mixed* policy ever beat both?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Always-$a$ from $X$ keeps you at $X$ forever — but $a$ from $X$ pays nothing.
Always-$b$ from $X$ moves you to $Y$, then $b$ from $Y$ moves you back to $X$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- Always-$a$ from $X$: stays at $X$, reward 0 forever. Reward-per-step = 0.
- Always-$b$ from $X$: $X \to Y \to X \to Y \ldots$, reward sequence
  $1, 0, 1, 0, \ldots$. Reward-per-step = $0.5$.

Mixed policies cannot beat $0.5$ here (every visit to $X$ pays in
expectation $\pi(b)$, which maxes at $1$ when $\pi(b) = 1$). But in
*partially observable* problems randomness genuinely helps: it prevents
adversaries (or symmetries) from exploiting your determinism. We will
return to this in lesson 19 ($\epsilon$-greedy exploration).
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Show that for any deterministic policy $\pi$ on a finite MDP, the
induced Markov chain has the same number of states $|S|$ but possibly fewer
*reachable* states from a given start. Give a small example.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A policy can refuse to take certain actions, leaving certain states
unreachable.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Example: a 2-state MDP $\{X, Y\}$ with $\pi(X) = a$ and $P(X \mid X, a) = 1$.
Starting from $X$, the agent never visits $Y$. The chain *as a graph*
still has $|S| = 2$ states, but the *reachable* set is $\{X\}$. This is
why "exploration" is a separate concern from "policy" — a policy can blind
itself to part of the world.
</details>

**S2.** A policy $\pi$ depends only on $s$ — not on the time step $t$.
Such policies are called **stationary**. Argue why, for the infinite-horizon
discounted-return objective on a finite MDP, restricting attention to
stationary deterministic policies *loses no expressive power* for the
optimal value (we will return to this in lesson 9).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Imagine the agent at $s$ at time $t$ vs at $s$ at time $t'$. What
differs?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Under the discount $\gamma$ and the Markov property, the *future* from
state $s$ at time $t$ is statistically identical (up to a global factor of
$\gamma^t$) to the future from $s$ at any other time. So the optimal
*action at $s$* is the same at every time — i.e. the optimal policy can
be stationary. And by the Bellman optimality theorem (lesson 9), the
optimal stationary policy can also be chosen *deterministic*. We get
"stationary deterministic" for free.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The word *policy* entered RL via operations research in the 1950s, not
computer science. Bellman, Howard, and Shapley were all using it before
Sutton and Barto's first edition. Howard's 1960 book *Dynamic Programming
and Markov Processes* used "policy" in nearly the modern sense — and
introduced *policy iteration*, which we will meet in lesson 14.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"A policy is the agent's brain."** Closer to: a policy is the agent's
   *output*. The brain (a neural net, a Q-table, a tree-search procedure)
   *computes* the policy from the current state. The policy itself is
   just a function from states to action distributions.
2. **"Stochastic policies are sloppy versions of deterministic ones."**
   Sometimes — but in partially observable problems, two-player games, and
   exploration, the optimal policy is *genuinely* stochastic. Randomness
   is a strategic tool, not a bug.
3. **"Policy and transition kernel are the same kind of thing."** They look
   similar (both are conditional distributions) but live in different
   places. The kernel $P(s' \mid s, a)$ is the *world*; the policy
   $\pi(a \mid s)$ is the *agent*.
:::

### Takeaways

1. A [policy](/glossary.qmd#policy-rl) is a conditional distribution
   $\pi(a \mid s)$ over actions given the current state. Deterministic
   policies are the corner case where one action gets all the mass.
2. Combining a policy with the MDP's kernel gives the **policy-induced
   transition kernel** $P^\pi(s' \mid s)$ — a plain Markov chain.
3. The collapse to a Markov chain is the magic that makes everything
   tractable: a century of Markov-chain theory now applies to your agent.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you, without notes, write down both the "policy as function"
   form and the "policy as conditional distribution" form, and explain
   when each is the right view?
2. Did you finish W1–S1 without peeking past *Hint 1*?
3. Can you describe in one sentence why "policy + MDP = Markov chain"
   simplifies the rest of RL?

- **3 yes:** continue to lesson 4 (returns and the discount).
- **2 yes:** redo P3 — the "when is randomness helpful?" question is the
  one that recurs in every later chapter.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* — Problems 3 and 4 contain the lesson.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **`mfrl.state-transitions`** — for the kernel $P(s' \mid s, a)$ that the
  policy is averaged against.

### What this unlocks

- **Lesson 4 — Trajectories, Returns, and the Discount.** Once a policy
  is fixed, you can sample trajectories and measure how much reward they
  accumulate.
- **Lesson 9 — Optimal Policies.** We will *compare* policies by the
  values they induce.
- **Lessons 19, 32–38** — exploration policies ($\epsilon$-greedy) and
  parameterised policies (REINFORCE, actor-critic) are all instances of
  the conditional-distribution view introduced here.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** A trained RL agent is, mathematically, just a policy. AlphaGo
is a policy. ChatGPT's RLHF-finetuned response distribution is a policy.
The neural network is the implementation; the policy is the interface.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Difficulty levels in a video game are often
*different policies for the same NPC*: easy = uniform-random, hard =
near-deterministic best-response. Same MDP, different $\pi$.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view; "the agent is a policy" is one of those
paragraphs.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), Chapter 1 §5. Cite freely; never paste Zhao's prose.
- Touchstones honoured: Russian school (Discover *is* the construction of
  $P^\pi$), Better Explained ("policy + MDP = Markov chain" is the Aha!
  framing).
:::
